item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An SCP that trolls you and puts you in cardiac arrest at the same time. Well that's just perfect isn't it?!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're right, and it's been fixed. Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2388-1 subjects are affected in three stages, each lasting approximately 64.

Should this be 64 hours? Idk I could be missing something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've had an idea for a skip half floating around in my head where the thing they're containing is a bootable drive that's actually super mundane, anyone could write it if they had the source code and it can be decompiled and examined, all of that. You'd have to boot to it directly because it requires more direct hardware access than it could get from an OS, but all it really does is a bunch of math or something and never does anything with the result. The problem is that on a specific model of cpu, the math it does shoots electrons around in a way that resembles a ritual that some programmer agreed upon with a demon, so when you run it with that cpu something something something it's only half an idea.

One of the things holding me back from writing it is that I want a computer-based skip that doesn't break my suspension of disbelief but I'm sure I'd break someone else's who understands that low-level hardware stuff better than I do

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Definitely, yes. That's been fixed, thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hi, I noticed that in the addendum the object is referred twice as SCP-2338 instead of SCP-2388. I guess its a mistake, yes?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really fucking like this, honestly. Like, it got an actual guffaw.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You could employ the time-honored technique of Subtracting Detail: Have it be a binary file CRUISE.bin that installs itself (by unknown means) into the appropriate executable type for its environment. Or have it be written in Java, and run on anything with a JVM. Have it recompile and re-link itself to the current runtime environment. Do some hand-waving and misdirection, and move the reader quickly along to the effects of the program, which is the real story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But programming is magic. Don't let anybody tell ya different! Seriously, I made my entire career on people thinking I was a sorcerer.

At any rate, I am also a programmer, and I am aware of how improbable this is, but it doesn't really bother me. The technical details of how this should work or could work are not really part of the story, and therefore entirely forgivable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's definitely improved by the edits, but unfortunately it's still not very interesting. Removal of flaws isn't enough to make an article good. The only part I feel favorable towards and not just neutral is the last line, and it isn't enough.

I largely concur with Gaffney's comment above, with the caveat that I think there's always a way to make this sort of thing interesting — or charming enough that we don't care — but that it's a higher bar, and this article doesn't reach it.

Like most articles that reach a minimum standard of being well-written and have been through a couple rewrites focused on improving flaws, it will probably kick around for a little while longer. I'd use that time to figure out ways to make it more interesting or entertaining.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Idk, my mental image when I read "This SCP makes you eat a sandwich with your eyeballs" is that the irises become Alien-style mouth-tongues that eat the sandwich.

In serious talk, I do understand your point. Programming is really really hard, and follows a very specific set of rules, and things that don't follow those rules break down. But honestly, to the average person (or maybe just to my brother, I don't have a lot of frame of reference when it comes to people), programming may as well be magic. It's like playing Starcraft as a biology nerd, I guess. You can very clearly tell that something is bullshit, but 99% of the people you complain to don't understand at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Taking a meme/joke and making it literal 1) has been done to death 2) is supremely uninteresting. This a boring repeat of the dozen-ish articles that cropped up when AWCY? first became A Thing People Liked, where you had a literal interpretation of a thing, followed by an explanatory note.

-1

e: I just realized how much this reminds me of SCP-1803. A lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Once I figured out it was caps lock being cruise control for cool, I really, really hoped it wasn't AWCY related. It should be no surprise that I was disappointed by the ending.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not saying this is a bad skip overall, but as I mentioned above, and in the actual section on computing, it's a serious, serious stumbling block.

It would have been better if there were either separate programs for each OS (I can believe an auto-run virus could be sufficiently advanced, given anomalous super-hipsters), or focus on one OS.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had considered this point for a while when first writing this; I had actually read your post about writing computer skips a few days before. I really wanted to find a work around, initially, but I realized that AWCY?, anomalous hipsters that they are, would certainly have a wide variety of operating systems in use. Some would be Apple fanboys because it's 'cool', some would ironically use Windows, and others would probably run personalized OS's. In the end, I decided that trying to work around it would end up complicating the article more than it would help.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, yeah.

Mostly because I know exactly how impossible that is. Something emitting radiation is just emitting radiation, that's the anomaly. Something that's working on all computer systems has to affect the motherboard, the hard drive, everything. Like, the entire system has to be fundamentally and physically changed for this to work.

Executable are especially bad. Unless the computer specifically is instructed to treat a file as an executable, it won't even try. And then, it has to follow the very highly specific encoding of that OS/instruction set.

It's like saying "This SCP makes you eat a sandwich with your eyeballs" there's no frame of reference.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Significantly better after the update.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh, I can grok: it breaks suspension for me when people hand-wave the chemistry too much. It's sort of like reading a line of unrealistic dialogue.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If AWCY took notes and inspiration from Uncyclopedia. I don't disagree with the last note.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wait a minute. We contain a homicidal statue that only moves when you're not looking, an alien lizard-thing that can regenerate from (and then adapt to) any damage, a round rock that turns mirrors into a portal to a really unpleasant alternate universe, and a being who may or may not be God.

And an anomalous program, one of the noted anomalies being that it runs no matter what OS is installed, is too far for you?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Indeed, but there's too much magic handwavey here for me, as a programmer.

Just my two cents.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Given that the code is apparently represented by the letter A repeated 29K times, the anomaly is more fundamental than universality.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The pacing could definitely do with an overhaul, I'm not a huge fan of how it describes the stages but I'd be lying if I said the last line didn't get me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This, like most computer related SCPs, falls in to the "universality fallacy" which is that an anomalous program could affect all computer systems.

While it may not be the universal view, the sheer impossibility of this, breaks my suspension hard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Didn't see the earlier versions, but I had to give this one a +1, especially after the note at the end. I feel much the same way about AWCY? - if you have to ask, you're not, no.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Alright, +2 isn't exactly failing, but it's not where I want this to be, so I've done a significant overhaul. Details in the top post.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's sort of tied to AWCY?, but more specifically, it's tied to Randomini's version of AWCY? from The Cool War series.

Explanation/spoilers
– hide block

*Overgang Dood, anartist computer programmer, in the signature and the username Wheres-my-car
*Rita Summers, another Cool War character, best known for her trained invisible spiders
*Nibman, an anartist of unspecified talent who is mentioned as a possible recruit to AWCY? at the end of The Cool War. Here, I'm assuming that he did get recruited, and took on the title of The Composer.

The intention here was that, while he doesn't like AWCY?, Overgang wouldn't want to make something that seriously injured anyone. He just wants to make their lives really annoying. Also (and this is almost certainly me trying too hard), 2929 is how you'd type out AWCY on an old flipphone.

E: Most of this is irrelevant now that I've updated it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's not that people dislike AWCY itself. Many of them just believe that it is rather overused or a cop out of a better story. An anomalous item appears, there's a note attached to it, signed by Wondertainment or AWCY or someone else, giving reasons for its creation or something like that.

Also, since AWCY is one out of many anartist groups and is simply one of the more outspoken and violent ones, some people want to see other anartist groups and what they have to offer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Overall I enjoyed the article and gave it a +1. But I could have done without the last two lines of the chat log, they felt a little over the top to me.

Personally I would like this better if it was directly tied to AWCY?1 but I think I'm in the minority of people on the site who actually like AWCY?.

Footnotes
1. Unless there is something in here tying it to AWCY? and I just missed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I guess we're still not cool yet, then.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, overall. The idea of it making folks yell so loud they damage their own hearing while protecting their throats struck me as slightly beyond the fundamentally mundane humor of the piece. I think it would be more effective for folks to be rendered almost unable to speak for fear of blowing their own voice, which would add a narrative knife-twist towards the all-caps speaking villains on the Internet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would have very much preferred a version of this where the anomaly itself is interesting enough to stand on its own. I don't care about these dbags, I care about weird magic capslock stuff. Surely there are all-caps analogues of things other than speech and typing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm. I added the log in later because people were telling me that it needed something more. Would it be any better if I dropped the second addendum completely, or does it need a different kind of thing to make it shine?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Stylistically well-written, but I've seen this kind of concept a lot (including the log at the end) and I didn't find this version particularly entertaining.

I do enjoy the central joke, but not enough to make me like it overall.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A story about people who use magical powers for puerile ends, told through convenient explanatory notes left around.

Nope.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mildly amusing. I would have preferred it to end after the description. For me, this is one of those cases where adding backstory is a detriment to the work.

Bickering, snide anartists are boring as all hell to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The file is consistently named CRUISE.EXE, so they can track that. Other than that, they're not so much tracking things based on that particular post as watching tech sites for questions about people's computers accidentally capitalizing things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like it, but I'm confused about this part of the containment procedures:

Foundation web crawlers have been updated with keywords related to SCP-2388 in an effort to find and contain additional instances of SCP-2388 online.

They only have a note to small forum post to go off of; so, how can they have a means of tracking instances of Capitalization?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Liked it before, like it now.

1+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to MrAnakinSpecter and Doc Expletive for looking at this, and double thanks to Randomini for critiquing and giving me permission to use Overgang Dood, albeit obliquely. removed in update

UPDATE: Significant changes have been made in response to feedback.
*Addendum 2 has been removed, along with the related containment update
*The item now has several stages of anomalous effects.
*Added new containment procedures to reflect the new effects.
*Acidverse references have been removed, as it no longer feels like something Overgang would do.
*More clearly states that this is related to Are We Cool Yet?.

Thank you all for helping me make this as good as it can be.

Spoliers
CAPS LOCK IS CRUISE CONTROL FOR COOL
